feedback.text: I am writing at a crucial moment for the future of the digital market to bring to your attention two serious problems that undermine consumer rights and freedom of expression, and hope that the Digital Fairness Act will provide a strong and definitive remedy for it. The first, and perhaps more insidious, problem threatening the digital market is censorship by financial intermediaries such as Visa and Mastercard. We are witnessing an alarming trend whereby digital distribution platforms such as Steam and Itch.io are forced to remove or hide perfectly legal video games. The pressure does not come from a judicial authority, but directly from payment agents. By invoking internal policies, which are often vague and linked to the protection of their own brand, these giants rely on the right to decide which content, although legal, can be sold on the European market. This dynamic constitutes a dangerous abuse of a dominant position. Payment operators act as e-commerce ‘bottlenecks’; the threat of revoking services forces platforms to be censured beforehand. This has a deterrent effect on creative freedom, particularly affecting independent developers, who do not have the bargaining power to oppose. The result is an approved market, where controversial but legal art is abolished not by law, but by the moral judgement of a private company. This is a direct attack on cultural diversity and freedom of expression, which are key principles of the European Union. The second problem, which has already mobilised millions of citizens through the Stop Killing Games initiative, is the deliberate destruction of regularly purchased video games. Consumers pay the full price for a product with a legitimate expectation of being able to use it forever. Instead, publishers stop operating by closing servers, making the software unusable. This practice turns a purchase into a long-term rental and destroys our digital cultural heritage. An emblematic case is ‘The Crew’ of Ubisoft, but the problem is systemic and undermines consumer trust in the entire digital market. If a game can be revoked today, tomorrow it could touch a film, e-book or software. A legal vacuum is created in which the consumer has all the duties of purchase, but no fundamental right to property. The Digital Fairness Act must act urgently on both fronts. We call for: Against the financial complaint: The introduction of a principle of neutrality of payment systems. For legal goods and services within the EU, financial intermediaries must not be able to refuse transactions or discriminate against merchants on the basis of the nature of the content offered. Democratic laws, not corporate policies, must govern the market. For the destruction of games: The legal obligation for publishers to provide ‘perpetuity patches’ that guarantee the full functionality of games, especially for single-player content, by deactivating online server dependencies once official support has been completed. Europe has the opportunity to establish itself as a global leader in protecting citizens’ digital rights, creating a truly fair, open and trustworthy digital single market.
